cards.question: Page 17 in 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview' (Introduction) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview Introduction Cost accounting, or internal accounting, refers to the subset of the accounting field that measures, classifies, and records the costs of items. A company can use a cost accountant, for example, to identify and segment various production costs for making good operating decisions. Generally, cost accounting refers to the financial transactions resulting from inventory movements. These movements can include: • Receipt of purchase orders (POs) • Shipment of customer orders • Issues to manufacturing orders (MOs) • Receipts from MOs • Time reported on MOs Variances reported on manufacturing orders • Transfers between warehouses • Cost of sales Infor M3 cost accounting tracks these transactions and assigns a value based on the following calculation: • (Unit quantity/time) x (unit cost/rate) This formula allows for detailed analysis of variances, inventory movements, and associate

cards.question: Page 18 in 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview' (Defining costing) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview Defining costing Defining costing means outlining and describing purchase and product costing within all kinds of enterprise processes. Costing always intends to analyze enterprise processes with regards to costs and efficiency. A company that sells products must be able to make financial decisions, such as: • Do we sell our products at a profit? Can we accept an order at a special price? Will an increase in sales result in increased profit? • Will it be more profitable to buy a product rather than manufacture it ourselves, or to process the product further before selling it? • Are our equipment and machinery cost effective? Is there wasted time, money, and effort in our processes? • What impact will production changes have on product cost? Questions such as these, which apply regardless of how products are acquired, are answered by means of product costing. Product costing is basically

cards.question: Page 19 in 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview' (Defining costing) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview Product and purchase costing flow Calculate product costs The company calculates the standard cost of a product or item based on a product costing model which represents the different types of costs to include in the calculation. Usually, this is done once a year or when a new item is introduced. After the calculation, you can view the calculated cost of the product broken down into material (component) costs, labor costs, tool costs, subcontracting costs, and calculated overhead costs. The detail included in the cost breakdown depends on the complexity of the product costing model selected. You can also calculate the cost of products and their variants, individual manufacturing orders, and work orders. cards.question: Page 20 in 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview' (Defining costing) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview Simulate and analyze product cost An important part of product and purchase costing is testing how changes to different parts of the costing model, including the cost rates, affect the results of the product cost. Therefore, a company probably would simulate such changes on a continuous basis to ensure that they can sell their products at a profit. You can compare changes between different product cost runs and between different costing types; for example, budget cost versus simulated cost versus financial standard cost. Depending on the proposed cost values of products, you can decide to make or buy the product or procure it from a subcontractor. You can also compare the costs of the same products, manufacturing orders, or work orders in different years. Infor M3 also lets you perform simulations based on sales price, profit, contribution margin, and contribution margin per machine hou

cards.question: Page 21 in 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview' (Defining costing) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview Update standard cost The procedure for updating the standard cost in financials is often referred to as “rolling the standard cost” or “doing a cost roll.” The following are two ways of doing that: • One method is to run a new product cost calculation using the costing type reserved for the financial standard cost and selecting a future date as costing date. This lets you use any function beginning with PCS2XX depending on if you’re rolling the cost for all items, variants, services, etc. • Another method is to first run a simulation, using a costing type not defined for financials and then copy it into the costing type reserved for the financial standard cost on Product Costing. Copy (PCS275). The advantage with this method is that it’s independent of any changes happening while a review is made of the impact of the new costing. cards.question: Page 22 in 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview' (Defining costing) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview Manage target costing Target costing is an optional part of the costing process. The purpose of target costing is to attain low costs and thus low prices. Throughout the target costing program, your aim is to decide “What should the new product cost be?” rather than “What does it cost?” Based on market research, you establish the target sales price and subtract the desired profit from it to yield the allowable cost. Then you can compare the product cost calculated by Infor M3 with the target price. Many companies attempt to do t his before the detailed design phase, since 70% to 80% of product costs are unchangeable after the drawings have left the designer’s hands. In addition to product cost, target costing options include cost-related figures such as target scrap rates and target lead times. You can enter and update target costs as well as cost driver factors such as lead time, order

cards.question: Page 23 in 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview' (Defining internal accounting) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview Defining internal accounting Internal accounting is all processes, methods, and procedures working together with the goal of the achievement of the organization’s objectives. The general idea of internal accounting is to ensure accuracy and validity of organizations financial statements, while also supplying the verification of the company’s operational effectiveness and efficiency. In Infor terms, internal accountings are all transactions which are not directly external. Internal accounting measures, classifies, and records costs such as production costs for decision making. The following are examples of financial transactions from inventory movements: • Receipt of purchase orders • Shipment of customer orders • Issues to manufacturing orders • Receipts from manufacturing orders • Time reported on manufacturing orders • Variances reported on manufacturing orders • Distributions of good

Transfer to GL (CAS960) ends abnormally, that is, if the FCR040 (work file for transfer) file is updated but not the general ledger, use Program Problem. Solve (CRS418) to reset the indicator for transfer of the internal account entries. All internal account entries can be reset based on a specified voucher number. The following values are then reset to zero in the CINACC table: • Accounting date • Year • Voucher number series • Voucher number A cancelling voucher is also created for the voucher number, according to your settings on Settings – General Ledger (CRS750/E). You can find the voucher number for the erroneous job on Transaction Work File. Restart Erroneous Jobs (GLS037), or on Transaction Work File. Restart Interrupted Jobs (GLS047). cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 1: Costing and internal accounting overview Costing Toolbox All businesses have a process to establish the cost for the products they are selling. Infor M3 has previously only offered the function Product Costing. Display (PCS300) to analyze product costs. In this course, the term product cost is used generally to describe the cost of any item in the Infor M3 system, regardless if it's manufactured, purchased or distributed. Infor M3 offers a completely enhanced functionality in the Costing Toolbox. It allows you to compare costs for different kinds of products not only side by side, but also across facilities and divisions. The normal security measures apply. If you are not authorized to work in a certain division, e.g., division BBB, you will not be able to display the cost in that division. Each division is used as a legal unit with its own general ledger, sales statistics, etc. A blank division, which must be defined, is used for central files such as the item, customer, and supplier files. A facility is created and connected to a main warehouse and division. A facility is necessary when defining the overall inventory structure for your company. A facility is also connected to the product structure and costing level. © Infor. All rights reserved. © Infor. All rights reserved. © Infor. All rights reserved. © Infor. cards.question: Page 38 in 'Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting' (System calendar and periods) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting System' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting System calendar and periods For Multiple Unit Coordination (MUC) configuration, a system calendar must be defined for each of the divisions included in the configuration. This means that a calendar must be defined for the central (blank) division, even if the division is not used to process information. When a date is entered in Infor M3, the system checks that the date has been defined in the system calendar. Up to five systems periods can be entered for each specified division and for the blank division. At least one seasonal curve can be entered for each specified division and for the blank division. System periods are used to enter different kinds of data that use different periods. © Infor. All rights reserved. © Infor. All rights reserved. © Infor. All rights reserved. © Infor. cards.question: Page 39 in 'Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting' (Number series) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting Number' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting Number series Number series are used for assigning unique identities to each individual order or transaction in Infor M3. They are significant because they are used in countless areas of Infor M3. Number series are beneficial to the end user as identification criteria. Journal number series When you exit a program that creates accounting transactions, one or several journals are printed, depending on the program. Examples are accounting journals, customer invoice journals, and supplier payment journals. Each journal is assigned a number from the journal number series. In the journal, every transaction receives a sequence number. This is done to give each transaction a unique identity in the general ledger and to order the transactions chronologically for Infor M3-internal purposes only. If you have selected to have transactions separated into external and internal transactions in Settings – Gene

cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting User, language, and currency settings To use Infor M3, each user must be defined as a user. The user profile in Infor M3 contains all the environmental information that is assigned to each user to set up the correct environment when the user starts a new session. This includes the default values for the company and division, language, date format, etc. for a user. The user can override some, but not all, settings within the session. Languages are very important and must be defined within Infor M3 before text can be used in applicable fields. Infor M3 provides a process for entering certain field descriptions in alternate languages in various master record programs. This functionality is particularly useful in instances where the system is implemented in multiple countries. cards.question: Page 42 in 'Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting' (Currencies in Infor M3) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting Currencies' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting Currencies in Infor M3 You can use any number of currencies in Infor M3. There are three key currency types: • Local currency: Local currency is the base, domestic currency used by the company and to which all transactions are converted in the general ledger. A value expressed in this currency is called local amount. • Foreign currency: Foreign currency is any other currency used. A value expressed in such a currency is called currency amount. • Third currency: Third currency is a currency with special status, such as the euro within the Monetary Union (EMU), or the US dollar. A third currency may be useful for reporting and comparison purposes, for example when you want to present your figures in a stable currency. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting Historic actual cost The historic actual cost can be used for auditing purposes and as an aid in assessing to which extent the company's product costs are accurate. Note: Currently, this procedure is a legal requirement in Brazil. As Infor M3 Business Engine (M3 BE) enables you to calculate a historic actual cost for purchased and manufactured items in arrears, regardless of their inventory accounting method. You can also update the general ledger with any variance between the current product cost and the historic actual cost. The historic actual cost represents the most accurate cost of the material or operation related to the product, since it is based on real costs incurred during the accounting period or the company -defined date range. Infor M3 BE applies the historic actual cost to the material or operation, throughout the product structure up to the top item, without changing the cost cal

cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 2: Configuring internal accounting Costing Toolbox Costing. Open Toolbox (PCS340) is a program used to consolidate existing simulations and display functionality in product costing. The background for this development was a fashion requirement to make enhancements to the purchase cost model simulation in Purchase Costing. Simulate (PPS295). Instead of making enhancements to Purchase Costing. Simulate (PPS295), Infor developed the Costing Toolbox. The list in Costing. Open Toolbox (PCS340) consists of a table with lines and columns. This provides independent cells for simulation and display of values. The toolbox model defines what is displayed on the different lines. In the model, you can also define values by cell. It is also possible to define if the cells on a specific line should be open for online input. Costing. At the same time, costs need to be as up to date as possible for costing types reserved for the current product cost and for simulations. cards.question: Page 69 in 'Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM) functions and accounting rules' (Overview) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM)' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM) functions and accounting rules Overview Infor M3 Finance has features and programs to assist in the daily processing and coding of business transactions that feed into general ledger. They are Financial Application Management (FAM) functions and accounting rules. FAM functions The best way to describe a FAM function is to think of it as a workflow. The purpose of the FAM function is to make the entry of new transactions as smooth and automated as possible. This is done by collecting settings or rules specific for certain types of entry. Transactions are recorded based on the methods, terms, and voucher number series specified in the FAM function selected. Accounting rules An accounting rule is a is a fully defined accounting string Infor M3 uses when account entries are automatically created in a financial activity. cards.question: Page 71 in 'Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM) functions and accounting rules' (FAM functions) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM)' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM) functions and accounting rules FAM functions Each FAM function consists of a main record and a detail record. FAM function main record The main record is an overall concept, an entry ID, for a certain function. Some main records consist of a name only. At least one detail record is attached to this main record. The simplest detail records only state which voucher number series and voucher name to use for a new transaction. FAM functions are either single (only one detail record is allowed) or multiple (several detail records are allowed). By adding several detail records to a main record, you create variants of the FAM function. In theory, you can add up to different variants for a multiple FAM function. This is very useful for recording customer payments. For example, you can tailor your entry to fit each payment document type in detail. Every main record has a four-character ID, such as

cards.question: Page 72 in 'Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM) functions and accounting rules' (Accounting rules) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM)' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM) functions and accounting rules Accounting rules Accounting events, types, and rules are predefined and are generated as part of the system configuration. Events and types are then married together in the function Accounting Rule. Set (CRS395). Once again, you simply ask the system to generate the accounting rules when conditions you set are met. Accounting rules can be set up to use fixed values for example, a control account or dynamic values, such as a product group. In addition, exceptions can be set up to apply in certain circumstances. An example can be the use of a different control account where the supplier forms part of a supplier group. Accounting rules are set up to avoid manual transaction entry and minimize key -in errors. This is critical because of the volume of transactions that occur in a fully integrated system. An accounting rule is a fully defined accounting string to

cards.goodAnswer: Examples of accounting events are reporting received goods, recording customer payments, and depreciating fixed assets. Every accounting event relating to results in at least two account entries: credit and debit. Here are some examples of events in Infor M3 that will generate financial transactions through the accounting rules: • AP10: Entering supplier invoices • OI20: Customer order invoicing • MM50: Goods are moved from one warehouse to another • PC10: A purchased product gets a new standard cost Accounting events are predefined in Infor M3, so they are downloaded during configuration or when upgrading the system. © Infor. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 3: Financial Application Management (FAM) functions and accounting rules Accounting type An accounting type is a collective ID for various types of account entries created during the accounting event, for example, the posting of sales revenue or accounts payable. Note that the same accounting type may be used for several events - invoicing a customer order is not the only event to generate sales revenue. You will find accounting type (revenue) in combination with service order invoices, sales of fixed assets, internal invoicing and so on. Examples: • Sales Revenue • Account Payable • Inventory Accounting types are predefined in Infor M3 and generated during configuration or at upgrade of the system. Accounting rule IDs The ID of an accounting rule consists of the combined IDs of the accounting event and accounting type. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing Overview A company that sells products must be able to make financial decisions, such as: • Are we selling our products at a profit after considering all costs associated with buying then? Can we accept an order at a special price or would we make a loss in doing so? Will an increase in sales result in increased profit? • Will it be more profitable to buy the item as a finished product, rather than manufacture it ourselves, or before selling it, to process the product further to add value? • Is our equipment and machinery cost effective? Is there wasted time, money, and effort in our processes? • What are the true costs for obtaining an item that we purchase? Is it what we pay the supplier or does it include other costs associated with the purchase? cards.question: Page 82 in 'Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing' (Purchase costing process) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing Purchase costing process The business flow of purchase costing begins by defining a total (landed cost) expressed as an average or standard cost of the item to be purchased. This typically begins with the price the company has either negotiated or feels they can procure the item for from the supplier. Other elements of the landed cost are added to this base cost, such as transportation cost, duty, tax, third-party processing costs, etc. This landed cost is the total amount the company thinks it will cost to procure the goods. However, circumstances can change. Some of those circumstances may include a change of supplier, purchasing from multiple suppliers with different prices, a change in the transportation method, distance, or rate. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing Supplier invoice match settings When referring to a three-way match, it pertains to these three areas: Components of three-way match A supplier invoice is matched to: • The goods receipt lines connected to the corresponding PO • The PO line, if there were no goods receipt transactions when matching took place • Both goods receipt lines and PO lines, if only some goods have been received If the matching was incomplete or not completely approved (such as when a supervising employee has to review it), the invoice is posted, normally to a clearing account. The Settings - Supplier Invoice Matching (APS900) function allows the business to set a limit when the three-way match will stop and indicate a price, or quantity variance. If the variance is less than the limit specified, it is automatically accounted for as a Purchase Price Variance (standard cost) or a change in inventory value (average co

The following identifies the fields and check boxes used to invoice match and describes the options available. Program/panel Field/check box Description Settings- Accounts Payable (APS905/F) Managing exchange rate gains/losses This check box is selected if any exchange rate gains or losses are to update the average cost of the purchase order’s item. Exchange rate gains/losses arise when the purchase order is in a foreign currency, and there is a difference in exchange rate between the date of the purchase order and the date of the goods receipt. cards.question: Page 94 in 'Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing' (Purchased item settings) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing Program/panel Field Description Item. Connect Facility (MMS003/E) Inv acc method This field specifies the inventory valuation method applied to the item. • Zero cost (0): The item has no cost (value) in this facility. • Standard cost (1): The standard cost is calculated per item/facility. • Average cost (2): This is the weighted average cost. It cannot be applied to manufactured items. • Dynamic product cost (3): This cost is only valid for manufactured items. • Actual cost (4): This cost requires that lot control is activated on the purchased items. • Simplified purchasing (5): Simplified purchasing items can only be used for items registered as non-inventory goods in the Inventory accounting field in Item. Connect Facility (MMS003/F) Cstng mod prod This field specifies what costing model is used for the product costing of an item. Normally, the costing model for product costing is set in Settings – Product Costing (PCS001). Item. Connect Facility (MMS003/F) Cstng mod sales This field specifies what costing model is used to calculate both basic prices and order-costed prices. Note: From a costing point of view, it is the acquisition method on the main warehouse for the facility that controls whether the item is regarded as purchased, manufactured, or distributed. cards.question: Page 107 in 'Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing' (Purchase costing model) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 4: Configuring purchase related costing Purchase costing model Purchase costing is the act of calculating the cost of acquiring items and to get it into the company's inventory. The items can either be the material required to manufacture a product or items to be sold off - the-shelf. The purchase costing model provides the framework, within which combines the costing elements together to calculate an inventory value of the item. The following is a summary of important settings used in to create your costing model and attach your costing elements. Costing Element. Open (PPS280) Costing elements are user-defined in Infor M3 (e.g., purchase price, inbound freight, tax and duties, etc.). When configuring an Infor M3 database, a company determines what costing elements are required to support the business and then sets these up accordingly. Costing elements are defined in Costing Element. Open (PPS280). Key fields and check boxes are

Enter Values (PPS282/E) Costing markup This check box controls whether costs, such as charges, should be included during purchase order entry. The parameter must be activated to use the costing model. Select the check box if the costing element percentages are positive, that is the element is for a markup. If you do not select the check box, then the costing element percentages are negative, that is a markdown. Note: The percentage is always entered without a sign. This check box indicates the percentage or value, which according to the costing operator, is to be used for the specified key combination in the purchase costing. The operator processes the following principal alternatives: • Price per defined unit • Percentage of a defined value The costing element determines if the markup is positive (addition) or negative (deduction).

cards.question: Page 124 in 'Lesson 5: Processing purchase costing and accounting' (Overview) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 5: Processing purchase costing and' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 5: Processing purchase costing and accounting Overview Purchase accounting is the process of recording internal accounting transactions when goods are received, and the supplier invoice is matched against the purchase order and goods receipt information. This process is more commonly known as the three-way match. A three-way match involves the following three documents: • The vendor's invoice which was received and becomes part of an organization's accounts payable if approved. • The purchase order that was prepared by the organization • The receiving report that was prepared by the organization You will carry out this process both for your average cost item and your standard cost item. The major difference between the two is that an average cost item will not create a purchase price variance (PPV) if the quantity being matched is still in inventory. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 5: Processing purchase costing and accounting Purchase costing This lesson provides an overview of the process for purchase costing in Infor M3. The process consists of two workflows: • One workflow for calculating and simulating the standard cost of an item • One workflow for costing on the purchase order level The company's accounting policies determine when to calculate the standard cost. New items are usually costed when they are introduced. The standard cost used as the financial standard is then often updated once a year only. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 5: Processing purchase costing and accounting Simulate cost There are two ways of simulating a standard cost and checking the impact of changed parameters in different business scenarios: • In the first alternative, you simulate values for an item with an optional costing model directly in Costing. Display/Simulate Model (PPS295). You can simulate the resulting standard cost, for example, if you change the delivery method from air to sea in a business scenario. You can simulate using other costing models, suppliers, and units of measures. The values can also be simulated in another currency at a certain exchange rate. Your simulations are not saved once you exit the program. • In the second alternative, you simulate a costing run for a purchased item and an optional costing model in Purchase/Distribution Costing. However, you can create a standard cost for all items, regardless of their inventory accounting method. The result is a "frozen" snapshot of the current cost at the time of the costing that you can use for simulations. To review the actual cost of a purchased item, you access Costing. Update Manually (PCS260). A requirement is that the cost to change is calculated on the current date or at a future date. Such invoices are entered in Accounts Payable and can be allocated across multiple purchase orders. Once invoices are received from suppliers and third-party agencies, you match the charges in the purchase order with the actual charges during invoice matching in Supplier Invoice. Match GR Line (APS360). You can also add new charges not included in the purchase order. For each new charge, you select a costing element. Open (PPS171) and select Actions > Account Entry (F11) allowing the ability to enter and update an accounting string. To access Planned Purchase Order. Open (PPS171), start Planned Purchase Order. Open (PPS170) and select Options > Change or Options > Display. This manually entered accounting string is used to create account entries PP10- and PP20-903. PP10: Goods receipt The basis for the account entries is created in one of the following situations: • Goods are reported as received in Purchase Order. Receive Goods (PPS300). • Goods receipts are deleted in Purc

cards.question: Page 175 in 'Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and accounting' (Overview) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and accounting Overview A company that operates in more than one place often needs to move inventory between various physical locations. It may be on a small scale, such as moving inventory to a parts warehouse located next door, or on a large scale, such as having a centralized production facility that supplies sales warehouses globally. Distribution costing is basically the function of calculating the company’s cost for moving goods internally between warehouses. It helps companies to identify, for example, if it is more profitable to centralize manufacturing and distribute the finished products or to decentralize and manufacture locally and therefore avoid the distribution freight costs. A calculated cost is essential to making decisions regarding pricing, product strategy, budgeting, and long-term investments. In addition, it also enables the company to compare the calculated cost to the co

cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and accounting Distribution costing process Distribution costing and accounting manages the process for distribution costing, that is, the costing of items received from other facilities within the company. This process primarily describes the calculation of standard costs. The standard cost of the distributed item is calculated and displayed in Product Costing. Display (PCS300) and Item. Connect Facility (MMS003/E). For manufactured items, the direct material cost for distributed items that are part of the product structure is reflected in the produ ct costing model. Cost information can be provided to management to: • Analyze overhead costs and charges. • Run inventory valuations based on the updated costs. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and accounting being produced by the company itself. Or, an item is distributed from one part of the company to another. In both scenarios, there are additional costs involved than just the cost of the goods itself. Since more companies acquire material from overseas sources, the process of determining the cost of an item is becoming increasingly difficult. In many cases, logistics-related costs have a big impact on the final cost of the end-product. Actually, the landed cost of an item is often much higher than just the cost of the goods. Note: With landed cost we mean the total cost of a product from one location to another, including cross-border fees like duties, taxes, freight costs, insurance costs, and quality i nspection costs, to name a few. This is why purchase costing and distribution costing play such a critical role in ensuring that the company maximizes its profit margin. Product

Defining rates For each costing element that represents a charge or a discount, you can select up to five predefined object types. In purchase/distribution costing, an object type represents a reason or a cause that triggers a different rate. For example, if you select warehouse, delivery method, and country as object types for a costing element representing an external freight cost, you can select different rates depending on a combination of these parameters. Items purchased for warehouse A and shipped from France by ai r have a different freight rate than the same items that are purchased for warehouse B and shipped from China by sea. cards.goodAnswer: Required costing elements in costing model In the simplest scenario, there are just two costing elements: one for the cost of goods – in this documentation called the costing base – and one for the total value of the costing. Note: For items with standard costing as inventory accounting method, the total represents the inventory value. In other words, a costing model must consist of at least the following costing elements: • A costing element with operator (retrieving the gross purchase price) or (retrieving the net purchase price) • A costing element with operator (the sum element representing the total value of the costing) Costing elements are defined in Costing Model. Each scenario is represented by a MUC trigger, which is stored in the tables together with the transactions. MUC trigger Scenario Goods are distributed between warehouses in the same facility. Goods are distributed between warehouses in different facilities but within the same division. Goods are distributed between warehouses in facilities in different divisions. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and accounting Distribution costing model Now you can start building your costing models out of the costing elements. It is theoretically possible to use the same costing model for both purchase costing and distribution costing. However, in practice it is probably more convenient to have separate costing models for each purpose. The costing model can be very simple, consisting only of costing elements for the costing base and a grand total, or quite complex with such values as overhead for procurement administration included. The distribution costing model is defined in Costing Model. Open (PPS285). Test costing model by simulating costs You test the costing model by simulating costs in different scenarios in Costing. cards.question: Page 194 in 'Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and accounting' (Internal transfer price) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and accounting Internal transfer price The distribution costing model with MUC allows us to control what the cost is going to be in the receiving warehouse or facility and can be either within a division or multiple divisions. You can define the internal price transfer models as we also need to be able to define the revenue in the sending warehouse/facility and division. Even if the item is transferred at cost, there is at least a revenue equal to the cost for the sending division. At a very minimum, the receiving warehouse needs to account their cost for the item they received. In other words, when you are shifting inventory either between your warehouses or your facilities or even between your divisions, you must be able to account for the costs, revenues and liabilities within each of these entities. How you do it is entirely up to the business requirements. There are three different cases t

cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 6: Configuring distribution costing and accounting Internal invoicing settings The basic parameters needed for internal invoicing between divisions are defined in Settings – Internal Invoicing (MFS010). This is only applicable for MUC cases. You will need to define which customer and supplier number these divisions represent in the two master files as well as whether any incoming internal supplier invoices are considered approved for payment or not. There is also a setting for the division used for consolidating the whole company as well as a flag to allow internal invoicing to be performed based on the delivery information. This prevents the receiving division from stalling the internal invoicing by not invoicing the customer. Invoicing the customer is normally a prerequisite for internal invoicing to take place. If needed, define a standard comment in Internal Invoice Text. Open (MFS015). A comment can be defined for all or a r

cards.question: Page 204 in 'Lesson 7: Processing distribution costing and accounting and Multiple Unit Coordination (MUC)' (Overview) - detail 1: Explain 'Lesson 7: Processing distribution costing and' and list the concrete admin rules, configuration points, or process steps from this page.
cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 7: Processing distribution costing and accounting and Multiple Unit Coordination (MUC) Overview Multiple Unit Coordination (MUC) allows you to use and configure Infor M3 in a multi -site organization. The function allows you to handle several legal entities, when a company consists of more than one legal entity. It can cover divisions in different countries, including the different currencies required. It also handles selling to customers or moving goods in different divisions and will account for all the internal invoicing, and intra company transactions that are needed in a seamless fashion. In its simplest form, an Infor M3 company consists of one legal unit (one division). There is one general ledger for financial purpose and there can be an optional number of facilities and warehouses used for physical flows. For many customers, however, one legal entity or business unit is not sufficient. Instead of setting up parallel comp

cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 7: Processing distribution costing and accounting and Multiple Unit Coordination (MUC) The following describes the MUC types. MUC # Description Both warehouses belong to the same facility, so the item has the same cost in both facilities. The inventory value may change from one account to another, but there is no internal revenue. Warehouses belong to different facilities, but the same division. Inventory value will shift from one account to another and internal revenue may be generated. Warehouses belong to different divisions. This is the most advanced case, where the DO behaves like an internal purchase/customer order. There may be internal revenue and therefore, an internal customer / supplier invoice is generated. We will use the second case (MUC 2), different facilities different division, for the distribution accounting workflow. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 8: Configuring product costing and accounting Overview The calculated product cost is essential when making decisions regarding pricing, product strategy, budgeting, and long-term investments. The following explains the different kinds of costs to consider in product costing. Recurring vs. non-recurring costs Costs are either recurring or non-recurring. Recurring costs are costs that are incurred from year to year for goods and services consumed, such as staff salaries. Non-recurring costs are not likely to happen again. Usually, only recurring costs are included in product costing. The main exception is when apportioning an imputed cost annually for a resource with a budget life longer than one year, for example, costs for a specific machine, land, or plant. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 8: Configuring product costing and accounting • Examples of non-production overhead costs (usually general costs that would remain the same regardless of production output) are: o Salaries of sales and marketing staff o Advertising costs o Administrative costs Fixed vs. variable costs Identifying which costs are fixed or variable in which circumstances is an important part of product costing. Fixed costs are costs that do not change when the volume of activity is changed, for example, rent for equipment. Variable costs instead do change; the cost increases when the volume of activity increases and decreases when the volume of activity decreases. An obvious example of a variable cost is the cost for materials used to manufacture a product. Traditionally, the cost per unit produced has depended on a high degree of variable costs. In modern manufacturing companies, fixed costs constitute an increasing part of costs due to increasing

cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 8: Configuring product costing and accounting Product settings Product is the term used in Infor M3 for items included in a product structure. A product refers to the superior item as described in a product structure; material refers to those items included in the superior item. In this connection, the terms product number and component number are the same as item number. An item can be both product and material, though not in the same product structure. As a product, t he item is made up of other items (material). While as material, the item is included in other items (products). As part of the exercises, the assembly time in the product structure is changed prior to creation of the manufacturing order to create a standard (planned labor usage variance). Also, the actual drilling time and amount of wrapping material reported will deviate from the standard to create both an actual labor usage variance and a material usage variance. Manufacturing order life cycle process Below is the process map for managing manufacturing orders: Process map for managing order manufacturing Create manufacturing order You can check and maintain your MO on Manufacturing Order. Note that the transaction price is the cost of the item on Product Costing. Display (PCS300). Manufacturing-related cost accounting settings The manufacturing accounting entries are created by the issuing of stock-material and components. This is triggered by reporting operations, work performed, and by the put-away of the finished product. These actions will also provide inventory for different scenarios. The following accounting event groups may be affected. PM10: Stock issues to Work In Progress (WIP) WIP account entries are created when material is issued to work in progress in Manufacturing Order. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 10: Order costing and managing internal accounting Overview The graphic below shows the general process for creating and correcting internal accounting transactions. This process is used to create a basis for cost accounting (internal accounting) by transferring internal account entries to the general ledger. Creating and correcting internal accounting transactions The result, the cost accounting base, is reviewed on Internal Account Entry. Open (CAS300). Here, all cost accounting transactions created in the CINACC file displays per the selected inquiry type. Internal account entries are transferred to the general ledger on Internal Account Entry. Transfer to GL (CAS960). The recommended transaction interval is the beginning of the accounting year to the date of transfer. It is then possible to catch any corrected internal account entries from previous runs. During the transfer, the validity of the transactions is checked against

cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 10: Order costing and managing internal accounting It is recommended that you schedule this function to run daily using the Infor M3 Job Scheduler at a time that there is no risk of a system backup being launched before the job has completed. If you are working on a project that has a high volume of transactions, you may want to break this job up into separate runs. Transactions from the cost accounting ledger are posted to the general ledger when Internal Account Entry. Transfer to GL (CAS960) is run. This job can be launched automatically when running Internal Account Entry. Create (CAS950), but if there are high volumes of transactions you may want to run this separately using the job scheduler. If you are conscious of the number of transactions generated by cost accounting, you can run Internal Account Entry. Transfer to GL (CAS960) less frequently than Internal Account Entry. cards.goodAnswer: Create (CAS950). Internal Account Entry. Transfer to GL (CAS960) Once the internal account entry is created on Internal Account Entry. Create (CAS950), use this program to transfer internal account entries to the general ledger in the form of a voucher. Internal account entries can be stock transactions, operation transactions, other transactions (that is, transactions that cannot be defined as stock- or operation transactions) or flagged-as-completed manufacturing orders and work orders for order costing. Create (CAS950/E) Transfer to FIM This check box indicates the transfer cost accounting transactions created to the financial system. If you select the check box, all transactions with error code and within the transaction date range set are transferred to the financial system. One voucher is created per batch job. If you do not select the check box, transactions are created with status (Not transferred). On Internal Account Entry. Open (CAS300), you can review and adjust the transactions, if needed. You can transfer these transactions later, in either this program (select the check box) or on Internal Account Entry. Transfer to GL (CAS960). Create (CAS950) is run. • If you are using standard costing, then there is very likely a variance between standard and actual cost. This variance is posted when Internal Account Entry. Create (CAS950) is run if the order is complete and is released for order costing. • If you have elected to have orders not released for order costing as a default, then you must release them individually on Order Costing. cards.goodAnswer: Lesson 10: Order costing and managing internal accounting Reviewing accounting entries You can review the cost accounting entries on-screen on Internal Account Entry. Open (CAS300) or as a report on Internal Account Entry. Print (CAS500). The on-screen review allows you to drill down to trace the original source of the transaction. If the entry has not yet been transferred to the general ledger, then you can adjust the accounting string , but you cannot change the value. In some cases, there may be an error with the entry and it must be corrected before it can be posted to the general ledger. Sorting order 7-error on Internal Account Entry. Open (CAS300) allows you to view errors. The most common reason for errors is incorrect setup in the accounting rules or master data setup issues that the accounting rules depend on. Accounting String. Simulate (CRS399) What information is contained in a FAM function detail record? All detail records contain information on the voucher number series used, the voucher name, and the valid date range or limits. Detail records may also contain settings unique to specific functions. These can include methods for calculating value-added tax (VAT) and cash discount respectively, and which panel layout to use for manual posting of transactions.
